subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank/IDB, Central Bank of The Bahamas, FATF, Freedom House, RSF) + international and Bahamian press.
A high-income, well-governed tourism-and-offshore-finance democracy on a USD peg, off the EU tax grey list (2024) and FATF-compliant - but with an existential hurricane tail (Dorian, 2019).

Standout features: credible institutions and an improving fiscal path (debt ~74% and falling on primary surpluses), against a fat catastrophe tail (~80% of land within ~1.5m of sea level), still-high (if falling) Nassau crime, and hard-edged treatment of Haitian migrants.

economic.notes: Low inflation and sound banks on a credible USD peg, with debt falling but distress risk without sustained effort.

fiscal_state.notes: Debt ~74% of GDP and falling, three consecutive primary surpluses, a new minimum top-up tax; 'high risk of debt distress' without sustained measures keeps the near-term modest. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

monetary_independence.notes: The BSD is pegged 1:1 to the USD - monetary policy is subordinated to defending the (credible) peg. (Central Bank of The Bahamas; IMF)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The BSD is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Low and imported via the peg - projected below 1% in 2025. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

banking_stability.notes: Well-capitalized, liquid domestic banks; a large but shrinking international book; pioneered the Sand Dollar CBDC. (IMF; Central Bank)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Narrow but high-value: tourism (esp. cruise) plus offshore finance, with little goods diversity and external-demand sensitivity. (IMF; World Bank)

institutional.notes: A functioning common-law democracy with credible data and a free press, with corruption and no FOI law.

rule_of_law.notes: A functioning common-law democracy with independent courts; persistent government/police-corruption concerns and no FOI law are the drags. (Freedom House 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: Credible, IMF-cited official data from the central bank and statistics office. (IMF)

civil_service_capacity.notes: A reasonably capable administration that delivered fiscal consolidation and AML reform, with small-state limits. (IMF; FATF)

press_freedom.notes: Free, pluralistic media, generally respected, with libel-law and small-market ownership constraints. (Freedom House; RSF)

political_social.notes: A mature two-party Westminster democracy, blemished by hard-edged treatment of Haitian migrants.

political_stability.notes: A stable, mature two-party Westminster democracy; the PLP re-elected May 2026 (the first incumbent re-election since 1997), peaceful transfers the norm. (AP/WaPo 2026)

civil_liberties.notes: Generally respected; the main blemish is harsh treatment of Haitian migrants/Bahamians of Haitian descent. (Freedom House 2025)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: A high-income destination with legal routes, but hard-edged, often due-process-light enforcement against Haitian migrants dominates the picture. (Freedom House; human-rights reporting)

social_cohesion.notes: Broadly cohesive, but inequality, anti-Haitian sentiment, and gang/retaliatory violence in Nassau are real strains. (Freedom House; RBPF)

demographics.notes: Stable and slowly aging, with an NHI-flagged aging-cost pressure and skilled emigration. (NHI; World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: A stable pro-US/Western partner, conflict-free, off the EU grey list with residual exchange controls.

alliance_reliability.notes: A stable, pro-US/Western Caribbean partner (CARICOM, close US ties) with no coercive dependency. (IMF; State Dept)

conflict_involvement.notes: No conflict or territorial threat; the main external exposure is Haitian-migration spillover, not war. (Freedom House)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Not sanctioned and off the EU grey list (2024), FATF-compliant; long-standing resident exchange controls are a structural friction. (FATF; IMF AREAER)

physical_practical.notes: Among the most climate-exposed states on earth, with chronic Nassau crime.

climate.notes: A low-lying limestone archipelago (~80% of land <1m) - the Dorian/Beryl precedent plus sea-level rise is near-existential. (IPCC AR6 SLR)

healthcare.notes: Decent access by regional standards, expanding NHI toward universal coverage, but with financing strain and overseas referral for complex care. (NHI; PAHO)

infrastructure.notes: Good in Nassau/Freeport and tourism zones, but archipelagic geography makes Family-Islands service and post-storm recovery hard. (IDB)

crime_safety.notes: Nassau violent crime is the chronic concern; murders fell 31% in 2025 but the rate (~21/100k) remains high. (RBPF; Nassau Guardian 2025)

disaster_insurance.notes: A functioning market plus sovereign CCRIF parametric cover, but the true catastrophe loss far exceeds payouts and reinsurance pricing is hardening. (CCRIF; WRI)

summary: A high-income, well-governed Westminster democracy with credible institutions, a stable USD peg, very low inflation, healthy banks, and a fiscal trajectory that is genuinely improving - debt down to ~74% of GDP on three straight primary surpluses, off the EU tax grey list and FATF-compliant - confirmed by a decisive May-2026 re-election of the Davis government. The dominant risk is physical, not political: with ~80% of its land barely above sea level and a Dorian-class hurricane able to wipe out years of progress in days, climate/disaster exposure defines the negative skew. Secondary drags are still-elevated (if falling) Nassau crime and hard-edged treatment of Haitian migrants. Solid expected value, fat catastrophe tail.
